title: शनि मङ्गलाष्टकम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री शनि मङ्गलाष्टकम् ॥

सूर्यपुत्राय शनये छायानन्दनसूनवे।
कृष्णवर्णाय मन्दाय श्रीशनैश्चराय मङ्गलम्॥ १॥

नीलाम्बरधराय नित्यं नीलाञ्जनसमप्रभाय।
काकवाहनसंस्थाय श्रीशनैश्चराय मङ्गलम्॥ २॥

शनिवारप्रियाय देवाय तिलतैलप्रियाय च।
नीलपुष्पसमर्चिताय श्रीशनैश्चराय मङ्गलम्॥ ३॥

कर्मफलप्रदात्रे च न्यायदण्डधराय च।
धर्मार्थकामदात्रे च श्रीशनैश्चराय मङ्गलम्॥ ४॥

साढेसातिनिवारकाय शनिदोषहराय च।
महादशाशुभकराय श्रीशनैश्चराय मङ्गलम्॥ ५॥

यमाग्रजाय धर्मज्ञाय तपस्विने महात्मने।
कुम्भमीनराशीशाय श्रीशनैश्चराय मङ्गलम्॥ ६॥

चित्रगुप्तसहायाय कालचक्रप्रवर्तिने।
सर्वजीवकर्मसाक्षिणे श्रीशनैश्चराय मङ्गलम्॥ ७॥

भक्तरक्षाकराय नित्यं सन्तोषमानसाय च।
सर्वपीडानिवारकाय श्रीशनैश्चराय मङ्गलम्॥ ८॥

शनिमङ्गलकं स्तोत्रं यः पठेच्छनिवासरे।
शनिपीडा न बाधेत सर्वसुखमवाप्नुयात्॥

॥ इति श्री शनि मङ्गलाष्टकम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Shani Mangalashtakam ||

Suryaputraya Shanaye Chhayanandanasunave |
Krishnavarnaya Mandaya Shri Shanaishcharaya Mangalam || 1 ||

Nilambaradharaya Nityam Nilanjansamaprabhaya |
Kakavahanasamsthaya Shri Shanaishcharaya Mangalam || 2 ||

Shanivara Priyaya Devaya Tilatailapriyaya Cha |
Nilapushpasamarchitaya Shri Shanaishcharaya Mangalam || 3 ||

Karmaphalapradatre Cha Nyayadandadharaya Cha |
Dharmarthakamadatre Cha Shri Shanaishcharaya Mangalam || 4 ||

Sadesatinivarakaya Shanidoshaharaya Cha |
MahadashaShubhakaraya Shri Shanaishcharaya Mangalam || 5 ||

Yamagrajaya Dharmajnaya Tapasvine Mahatmane |
KumbhameenaRashishaya Shri Shanaishcharaya Mangalam || 6 ||

Chitraguptasahayaya Kalachakrapravartine |
SarvajeevakArmasakshine Shri Shanaishcharaya Mangalam || 7 ||

BhaktarakShakaraya Nityam Santoshamanasaya Cha |
Sarvapeedanivarakaya Shri Shanaishcharaya Mangalam || 8 ||

Shanimangalakam Stotram Yah Pathechchhanivasare |
Shanipeeda Na Badheta Sarvasukhaam Avapnuyat ||

|| Iti Shri Shani Mangalashtakam Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
सूर्यपुत्र, छायानन्दन, कृष्णवर्ण, मन्द गति शनैश्चर को मंगल।

नीलाम्बरधारी, काकवाहन, शनिवार प्रिय, तिलतैल प्रिय शनि को मंगल।

कर्मफलप्रदाता, न्यायदण्डधर, साढ़ेसाती निवारक शनि को मंगल।

यमाग्रज, धर्मज्ञ, तपस्वी, कर्मसाक्षी, भक्तरक्षक शनि को मंगल।

शनिवार को पढ़ने से शनिपीड़ा नहीं होती और सर्वसुख प्राप्त होता है।
benefits: Eight auspicious verses to please Lord Shani
Traditionally recited for removal of sade sati and shani dasha afflictions
Invokes shani's blessings for karma and justice
Traditionally invoked for protection from all shani-related planetary troubles
Must recite on Saturdays for maximum benefit
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Shani Dev, Shani Mangalashtakam (शनि मङ्गलाष्टकम्) stands as an eight-verse devotional hymn. Attributed to Skanda Purana, this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Regular recitation is believed to must recite on Saturdays for maximum benefit, protect from all Shani-related planetary troubles, and remove Sade Sati and Shani Dasha afflictions. This prayer is traditionally recited on Saturday (Shanivar) and during Shani Jayanti and Shani Amavasya to invoke divine grace. At 8 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 3 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Shani Mangalashtakam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Shani Mangalashtakam in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Eight auspicious verses to remove Sade Sati and Shani afflictions.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Shani Mangalashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shani Mangalashtakam (शनि मङ्गलाष्टकम्) is an eight-verse Sanskrit auspicious hymn (mangalashtakam — eight verses ending with the auspicious refrain mangalam) addressed to Shri Shanaishchara — Lord Shani, the slow-moving graha (planet Saturn), the son of Surya and Chhaya, the elder brother of Yama, the regent of justice, karma and consequence. The verses describe Shani in classical iconography — son of Surya (Suryaputra), born of Chhaya (Chhayanandana), of dark complexion (krishnavarna), slow-moving (manda), clad in blue garments (nilambara-dhara), shining like collyrium (nilanjana-sama-prabha), riding the crow (kakavahana), beloved of Saturday (Shanivara-priya), pleased with sesame oil (tila-taila-priya), worshipped with blue flowers, the bestower of karma-phala, the just judge (nyaya-danda-dhara), and especially the remover of Sade Sati and Shani-dosha. (Source: classical Shani upasana tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Shani Mangalashtakam be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shani Mangalashtakam is most powerful when chanted on Saturday (Shanivar — Shani's principal day) at sunset, before a Shani idol with offerings of sesame oil (til ka tel), blue or black flowers (especially nilakurinji or aparajita), black urad dal, and a black cloth or chhata (umbrella). The supreme festival is Shani Jayanti (Vaishakha Amavasya — Shani's birth anniversary), Shani Amavasya (any Saturday falling on Amavasya), the Saturdays of Shravan, throughout periods of Sade Sati (the 7.5-year transit of Shani through the moon-sign and adjacent signs), Dhaiya (the 2.5-year transit through 4th and 8th from moon), Shani Mahadasha (the 19-year planetary period of Shani), and during Shani's transits over key chart points. Visiting Shani Shingnapur (Maharashtra) or Tirunallar Saneeswaran temple (Tamil Nadu) on Saturday with the recitation is especially powerful. (Sources: Drikpanchang Shani parva calendar; Jyotisha grah-shanti tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Who is Lord Shani and why is he called Mandagati?
seoFaqs.answer: Lord Shani (Shanaishchara, literally the slow-mover from shanaih = slowly + char = to move) is the divine regent of the planet Saturn, the slowest-moving of the visible planets — taking approximately 30 years to complete one zodiacal revolution and 2.5 years per sign. He is the son of Surya the Sun and Chhaya the shadow-wife (since Sanjna, Surya's true wife, withdrew unable to bear his fierce light), making him the half-brother of Yama Dharmaraja (the lord of death and karma-judgement). Shani inherited from his parentage the dual qualities of cosmic brilliance and shadowed delay — and presides over karma-phala (the fruit of past actions), longevity, ascetic discipline, justice, hardship, lessons-through-suffering, longevity and the eventual liberation that comes through karmic exhaustion. He is the karaka (significator) of duhkha (sorrow), tapas (austerity), and Moksha. (Sources: Brahma Vaivarta Purana; Skanda Purana; Suryaputra Shani Mahatmya.)

seoFaqs.question: How does the Shani Mangalashtakam help with Sade Sati and Shani dosha?
seoFaqs.answer: Sade Sati is the 7.5-year transit of Shani through the 12th, 1st (moon-sign) and 2nd house from the natal moon — affecting every native once or twice in a lifetime and traditionally bringing tests in health, finance, family and career. Dhaiya (Ashtama Shani / Kantaka Shani) is Shani's 2.5-year transit through the 4th or 8th from the moon. Shani Mahadasha is the 19-year vimshottari planetary period of Shani. The Shani Mangalashtakam's fifth verse explicitly invokes Shani as Sade-sati-nivarakaya — the remover of Sade Sati, Shani-dosha-haraya — the destroyer of Shani-dosha, and Mahadasha-shubhakaraya — the maker of an auspicious Mahadasha. Reciting it on Saturdays through the affected period, combined with offering tila-taila to a Shani vigraha or peepal tree, is the classical jyotisha remedy for transforming Shani's tests into purifying tapas. (Sources: classical Jyotisha graha-shanti; Shani Mahatmya.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the difference between the Shani Mangalashtakam and the Dasaratha Shani Stotra?
seoFaqs.answer: Both are sacred hymns to Lord Shani but differ in source, length and traditional purpose. The Dasaratha Shani Stotra (also called the Dasharatha Krita Shani Stotra) is the famous 10-verse Sanskrit hymn composed by King Dasharatha — Rama's father — in the Padma Purana when he confronted Shani in the heavens to prevent the planet's transit into Rohini nakshatra (which would have brought a 12-year drought); it is widely recited as a powerful Shani-shanti remedy with 28 names of Shani. The Shani Mangalashtakam is a shorter eight-verse mangalashtakam in the auspicious benediction format, focusing on Shani's pleasing aspects (Suryaputra, Mandagati, Krishnavarna, Sade-sati-nivaraka) and ending each verse with mangalam. The Dasaratha Stotra is more potent for active Sade Sati relief; the Mangalashtakam is the daily Saturday auspicious recitation. (Sources: Padma Purana Uttara Khanda; classical Shani stotra corpus.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Shani Mangalashtakam grant?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition attributes the following blessings to sincere recitation of the Shani Mangalashtakam: (1) Sade-sati-nivarana — relief during the 7.5-year Sade Sati transit; (2) Shani-dosha-hara — removal of all Shani afflictions in the natal chart (debilitation, combust Shani, malefic conjunctions); (3) Mahadasha-shubha — transformation of the 19-year Shani Mahadasha into a period of growth and stability; (4) longevity, slow-but-permanent prosperity, professional stability, and protection of property; (5) protection from accidents, chronic illness, joint problems, depression, and karmic suffering; (6) blessings for those in service-oriented professions (Shani rules service, labor, oil, iron, leather, justice); (7) granting of patience, perseverance, discipline and tapas-shakti; and (8) ultimately moksha through the exhaustion of karma — for Shani is the karma-phaladata, the bestower of karmic fruit who eventually liberates. (Source: Shani upasana tradition; Jyotisha phalashruti.)
